title: Environment and Energy Economics
gist: This is the NBER Environment and Energy Economics program landing page, which LISTS many recent working papers with short abstracts rather than presenting a single paper. Notable individual findings span EV/tariff policy design, data-center energy externalities, shale gas consumer savings, disaster/insurance pricing, and pollution-substitution effects; each insight below captures one paper's finding.

insights.claim: An NBER working paper (35334) by Barwick et al. argues optimal trade and industrial policy for the EV transition combines tariffs with revenue-funded subsidies when governments pursue environmental objectives alongside national welfare.
insights.speaker: NBER
insights.so_what: Frames how EV import tariffs may be paired with subsidies — relevant to auto/battery supply-chain and IRA-style policy bets.
insights.quote: From Trade War to Green Transition: Optimal Electric Vehicle Tariffs with Revenue-Funded Subsidies

insights.claim: An NBER working paper (34871) by Barwick, Li & Xia provides formal economic quantification of EV 'range anxiety,' long cited as a major adoption barrier but previously not rigorously measured.
insights.speaker: NBER
insights.so_what: Quantified range anxiety informs charging-infrastructure investment theses and EV adoption-curve forecasts.
insights.quote: Range anxiety, the fear of depleting battery before reaching a charging station, is often cited as a major barrier to electric vehicle (EV) adoption

insights.claim: An NBER working paper by Asensio et al. finds real-time charging data could accelerate EV adoption, with charging infrastructure reliability and accessibility remaining significant impediments to the transition.
insights.speaker: NBER
insights.so_what: Charger reliability (not just count) is the binding constraint — points to value in charging-network uptime/data plays.
insights.quote: Charging infrastructure reliability and accessibility remain significant impediments in the transition to electric vehicles

themes.theme: Data centers and AI energy footprint

insights.claim: An NBER working paper (35194) by Alvarez et al. uses a shift-share approach to study how AI-driven data-center buildout affects local economies, noting rapid AI diffusion is accelerating compute demand and data-center investment.
insights.speaker: NBER
insights.so_what: Academic confirmation of AI compute demand reshaping local economies supports data-center/power-demand theses.
insights.quote: The rapid diffusion of artificial intelligence (AI) is intensifying demand for compute, accelerating investment in data centers
insights.locator: Working Paper 35194 — Data Centers and Local Economies

insights.claim: An NBER working paper (35100) by Nicholas Z. Muller quantifies environmental externalities of US data-center electricity use using facility-level data for roughly 2,800 operational data centers in 2025.
insights.speaker: NBER
insights.so_what: Puts a monetary damage figure on data-center emissions — feeds regulatory/ESG risk for hyperscaler power demand.
insights.quote: Using facility-level data for approximately 2,800 operational data centers in 2025
insights.locator: Working Paper 35100 — Measuring the Impact of Data Centers

themes.theme: Energy supply: shale, solar, oil/gas liabilities

insights.claim: An NBER working paper (35245) by Lucas W. Davis estimates how much shale gas has saved US consumers, recalling that mid-2000s US natural gas production had been flat for a decade with the US planning to import LNG before hydraulic fracturing and horizontal drilling reversed the trend.
insights.speaker: NBER
insights.so_what: Quantifies the consumer-surplus magnitude of the shale revolution — useful baseline for US gas-cost competitiveness.
insights.quote: Advances in hydraulic fracturing and horizontal drilling caused U.S
insights.locator: Working Paper 35245 — How Much Has Shale Gas Saved

insights.claim: An NBER working paper (34893) by Banares-Sanchez et al. studies Chinese solar subsidies, finding their implementation by city-regions went alongside massive expansion of the sector, examining whether clean-energy industrial policy can raise growth and welfare while cutting carbon.
insights.speaker: NBER
insights.so_what: Evidence on returns to Chinese solar subsidies bears on global solar oversupply and clean-energy industrial-policy debates.
insights.quote: Ray of Hope? China and the Rise of Solar Energy

insights.claim: An NBER working paper (34961) by Armitage, Boomhower & Hausman finds asset reallocation in US oil and gas production can drive rent-seeking via differences in expected environmental-liability costs, not just efficiency gains.
insights.speaker: NBER
insights.so_what: Flags that well/asset transfers can shift cleanup liabilities to weaker firms — a credit/ESG risk in O&G M&A.
insights.quote: it can also lead to distortions via rent-seeking
insights.locator: Working Paper 34961 — Cutting Costs or Cutting Corners

insights.claim: An NBER working paper (34905) by Gilje et al. shows the April 20, 2020 settlement of WTI crude futures below zero for the first time was catalyzed by Cushing, Oklahoma delivery/storage constraints.
insights.speaker: NBER
insights.so_what: Mechanism behind negative oil prices is a benchmark-design/storage risk relevant to commodity futures positioning.
insights.quote: the West Texas Intermediate (WTI) futures contract for delivery in Cushing, Oklahoma, settled below zero for the first time in history

insights.claim: An NBER working paper (35087) by Graff Zivin et al. documents a pollutant-substitution effect from China's air-monitoring rollout: PM fell significantly while ozone surged, as local governments facing binding PM targets engaged in strategic behavior.
insights.speaker: NBER
insights.so_what: Incomplete pollution metrics distort incentives — a caution for ESG/regulatory scoring that targets single pollutants.
insights.quote: we document a pollutant substitution effect: PM. fell significantly, yet O surged
insights.locator: Working Paper 35087 — The Unseen Costs of Blue Skies

insights.claim: An NBER working paper (35317) by Keller, Knittel et al. estimates the causal effect of PM pollution on internal migration between US commuting zones over 2005-2019 using a dyadic migration model.
insights.speaker: NBER
insights.so_what: Pollution-driven migration affects regional housing/labor markets — a slow structural demographic signal.
insights.quote: We estimate the effect of PM. pollution on migration between commuting zones in the United States from 2005-2019
insights.locator: Working Paper 35317 — Air Pollution and Internal Migration

insights.claim: An NBER working paper (35288) by Keiser et al. reports the US has spent $2 trillion on safe drinking water since the 1974 Safe Drinking Water Act, yet drinking water for 10-20 percent of Americans still violates standards.
insights.speaker: NBER
insights.so_what: Persistent water-quality gaps despite huge spend point to ongoing water-infrastructure investment need.
insights.quote: the U.S. has spent $2 trillion to provide safe drinking water, yet drinking water for 1020 percent of Americans violates standards

themes.theme: Climate risk pricing, insurance, and finance

insights.claim: An NBER working paper (34848) by Blonz et al. uses 70 million policies linked to mortgages to show credit scores affect homeowners-insurance premiums as much as disaster risk, with low-credit homeowners paying 24% more for identical coverage.
insights.speaker: NBER
insights.so_what: Credit-score-driven insurance pricing is a hidden affordability/regulatory risk for insurers and mortgage holders.
insights.quote: Homeowners with low credit pay 24% more for identical coverage than highcredit score homeowners

insights.claim: An NBER working paper (35307) by Casey, Meng & Rudik builds a quantitative trade model comparing US carbon tariffs with and without a domestic carbon tax, examining whether carbon import tariffs work as standalone climate policy.
insights.speaker: NBER
insights.so_what: Carbon-border-adjustment design directly affects carbon-intensive trade-exposed sectors.
insights.quote: Carbon import tariffs, traditionally considered a complement to domestic climate policy, are increasingly proposed as standalone policies
insights.locator: Working Paper 35307 — Are carbon tariffs climate policy?

insights.claim: An NBER working paper (34828) by Hanlon et al. hypothesizes that financial exposure to green-transition energy stocks can induce learning and greater individual support for climate-mitigation policy.
insights.speaker: NBER
insights.so_what: Links portfolio exposure to climate-policy preferences — a behavioral channel for ESG flows and political alignment.
insights.quote: financial exposure to energy stocks central to the green transition can induce learning and greater support for climate mitigation policies

insights.claim: An NBER working paper (35066) by Gonzales, Ito & Reguant studies how nationwide blackouts affect the economy, noting growing energy demand, renewable integration, and natural disasters increase large-scale blackout risk while the economic impacts remain largely unknown.
insights.speaker: NBER
insights.so_what: Grid-reliability economics matter as load growth (AI, electrification) raises blackout tail risk for utilities and industry.
insights.quote: Electricity reliability is a central challenge for the energy transition
